headline: Maternal Citizenship Amendment Passed 2024; March 2025 Court Settlement Covered Pre-Amendment Cases
context: Parliament passed the Constitution (Amendment) Bill 2024 in October-December 2024, enabling overseas-born children of Malaysian mothers to acquire citizenship after commencement. A March 2025 Federal Court consent order covered a defined class of pre-amendment cases.

cards.big: Parliament passed the maternal-citizenship amendment in 2024. Pre-amendment children needed a separate Federal Court settlement to be covered.
cards.sub: Suriani Kempe v Kerajaan Malaysia ran 2020 to 2025. The legislative fix arrived in October-December 2024.

cards.big: The Dewan Rakyat passed the Constitution (Amendment) Bill 2024 on 17 October 2024; reporting and party statements place the Dewan Negara vote on 3 December 2024.
cards.sub: The amendment replaces father-only language with parent-inclusive. Automatic citizenship operates from commencement, not retroactively.

cards.big: On 10 March 2025, a Federal Court bench endorsed a consent order between Family Frontiers and the government for pre-amendment cases.
cards.sub: Children under 18 born overseas to Malaysian mothers and foreign fathers before the amendment can now apply for citizenship under Article 15(2).

cards.big: Residual gaps remain: non-retroactivity, pre-implementation births, adults over 18, and stateless local-born children outside the settlement class.
cards.sub: Family Frontiers and other advocacy groups continue to press for further reforms beyond the headline amendment.

cards.big: The story is no longer 'will the government recognise mothers'. It is which residual gaps remain after the legislative and judicial fixes settled the main case.
The amendment plus the March 2025 settlement substantially addresses the central injustice. The remaining work is on the cases the headline reform did not touch.
